children.name: Todus
children.description: Tiny, brilliantly-coloured birds endemic to the Caribbean islands, with vivid green upperparts, a red throat, and a long, flattened bill adapted for sallying after insects. Todies are the closest New World relatives of the kingfishers and motmots. Despite their diminutive size, they have loud, buzzing calls and are feisty defenders of their territories.

children.name: Todus multicolor
children.description: Endemic to Cuba and adjacent islands, the Cuban Tody is a miniature jewel of the forest with bright green upperparts, a red throat, a white chest, and pinkish flanks. It is remarkably tame and often observed at close range, sitting motionless on a low perch before darting out to catch insects. Its buzzy, insect-like call is a characteristic sound of Cuban forests.

children.name: Todus todus
children.description: Endemic to Jamaica, where it is known locally as the 'robin redbreast'. It is similar to the Cuban Tody but with a more extensive red throat extending onto the upper breast. It inhabits all forest types on the island, from dry limestone forests to wet montane rainforests.

children.name: Todus subulatus
children.description: Endemic to the island of Hispaniola (Haiti and Dominican Republic), where it is one of two tody species. It has a notably broader bill than its congener, the Narrow-billed Tody, adapted for catching larger prey. It is more common in the lowlands but overlaps with the Narrow-billed Tody in some mid-elevation forests.

children.name: Todus mexicanus
children.description: Endemic to Puerto Rico, where it is an iconic endemic known locally as the 'san pedrito'. Despite its scientific name, it occurs only in Puerto Rico and not in Mexico. It is the smallest Puerto Rican bird and is found in all forest types from sea level to the highest peaks.

children.name: Todus angustirostris
children.description: Endemic to the island of Hispaniola (Haiti and Dominican Republic), where it is the second tody species alongside the Broad-billed Tody. It has a notably narrower bill, adapted for catching smaller insect prey. It is more common in montane forests at higher elevations than its congener, occurring up to 2,500 metres in the pine and cloud forests of the interior.
